draft.introduction: Isaiah 53 describes the suffering servant’s death and vindication—despised, rejected, led like a lamb to slaughter. The Ethiopian eunuch was reading this passage when Philip encountered him (Acts 8:32).

translation.text: Who has believed what he has heard from us? And to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed? For he grew up before him like a young plant, and like a root out of dry ground; he had no form or majesty that we should look at him. He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.
translation.original_text: κύριε τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν

translation.text: He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter, and like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.
translation.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς διὰ τὸ κεκακῶσθαι οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα

translator_notes.note: The lamb imagery is quoted directly in Acts 8:32-33. The association with Passover sacrifice predates Christianity; the Dead Sea Scrolls suggest messianic readings of this passage already existed in Second Temple Judaism.
